event: Hunter-gatherers begin settling the land as the ice sheet retreats at the end of the last glacial period.
Neolithic farming communities spread across southern Scandinavia, leaving megalithic tombs.
The Nordic Bronze Age flourishes, producing rock carvings such as those at Tanum.
The Iron Age begins as iron-working spreads through Scandinavian societies.
The Roman historian Tacitus mentions the Suiones (Swedes) in his work Germania.
The Vendel Period sees the rise of powerful chieftains and rich boat burials near Old Uppsala.
The Viking Age begins as Scandinavian raiders and traders venture across Europe and eastward.
The Frankish missionary Ansgar arrives at the trading town of Birka to spread Christianity.
Erik the Victorious reigns as one of the first historically attested kings of the Swedes.
King Olof Skotkonung is baptized, becoming the first Christian king of Sweden.
The Archdiocese of Uppsala is established, organizing the Swedish church.
Birger Jarl founds Stockholm and consolidates royal power for the Bjelbo dynasty.
King Magnus Ladulas issues ordinances establishing the Swedish nobility and feudal privileges.
King Magnus Eriksson promulgates a national law code unifying Sweden's provincial laws.
The Kalmar Union unites Sweden, Denmark, and Norway under a single monarch.
Uppsala University, the oldest in Scandinavia, is founded.
The Stockholm Bloodbath sees Danish king Christian II execute scores of Swedish nobles.
Gustav Vasa is elected king, breaking the Kalmar Union and founding an independent Swedish state.
Gustav Vasa launches the Reformation, establishing a Lutheran national church and seizing church lands.
Gustavus Adolphus ascends the throne and begins transforming Sweden into a great military power.
Sweden enters the Thirty Years' War, intervening decisively on the Protestant side.
King Gustavus Adolphus is killed at the Battle of Lutzen.
The Peace of Westphalia confirms Sweden as a dominant power in the Baltic region.
The Treaty of Roskilde transfers the southern provinces of Scania, Halland, and Blekinge to Sweden.
The Great Northern War begins, pitting Sweden against a coalition led by Russia.
Sweden suffers a catastrophic defeat at the Battle of Poltava, ending its military dominance.
The Treaty of Nystad ends the Great Northern War, marking the decline of the Swedish Empire.
The Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences is founded, advancing figures like Linnaeus and Celsius.
Gustav III seizes power in a coup, ending the parliamentary Age of Liberty and restoring royal authority.
Sweden loses Finland to Russia and adopts a new constitution establishing separation of powers.
Sweden enters a union with Norway after the Napoleonic Wars, its last military conflict.
Jean-Baptiste Bernadotte becomes King Charles XIV John, founding the current royal dynasty.
Sweden introduces compulsory primary education for all children.
A bicameral parliament (Riksdag) replaces the old four-estate assembly.
The first Nobel Prizes are awarded in Stockholm under Alfred Nobel's bequest.
The union with Norway is peacefully dissolved, granting Norway full independence.
Sweden declares neutrality at the outbreak of World War I, a stance it maintains.
Universal and equal suffrage is achieved, with women voting in national elections for the first time.
The Social Democrats take power and begin building the modern welfare state, the 'folkhemmet'.
Sweden maintains neutrality during World War II while making concessions to Germany.
Sweden joins the United Nations.
Swede Dag Hammarskjold becomes Secretary-General of the United Nations.
A new Instrument of Government reduces the monarch to a purely ceremonial role.
Prime Minister Olof Palme is assassinated in central Stockholm.
Sweden joins the European Union.
The Oresund Bridge opens, linking Sweden to Denmark by road and rail.
Swedish voters reject adopting the euro in a national referendum.
Sweden joins NATO, ending more than two centuries of military non-alignment.
